sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a Doppler ultrasound probe used to estimate flow speed in tubes carrying a blood-mimicking fluid to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that the Doppler shift increases with source frequency and with the component of target velocity along the beam. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as Δf is proportional to v cosθ when wave speed and transmitted frequency are fixed. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved increasing fluid speed while holding beam angle and transmitted frequency constant. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: measured frequency shift increased approximately linearly with flow speed. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that beam angle, fluid composition, probe frequency, and sound-speed calibration were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that a larger Doppler shift means the ultrasound wave itself travels faster through the same fluid. Another was that Doppler shifts occur only for electromagnetic waves and not for mechanical sound waves. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to Doppler flow ultrasound, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the Doppler flow ultrasound experiment?

choices.A: A larger doppler shift means the ultrasound wave itself travels faster through the same fluid
choices.B: The result is best explained by a larger velocity component along the ultrasound beam producing a larger frequency shift
choices.C: Doppler shifts occur only for electromagnetic waves and not for mechanical sound waves, even though the passage states that beam angle was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that A larger doppler shift means the ultrasound wave itself travels faster through the same fluid conflicts with the stated relationship, Δf is proportional to v cosθ when wave speed and transmitted frequency are fixed, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that measured frequency shift increased approximately linearly with flow speed and states that beam angle, fluid composition, probe frequency, and sound-speed calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.B: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as Δf is proportional to v cosθ when wave speed and transmitted frequency are fixed, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, measured frequency shift increased approximately linearly with flow speed, while beam angle, fluid composition, probe frequency, and sound-speed calibration were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The result is best explained by a larger velocity component along the ultrasound beam producing a larger frequency shift. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Doppler shifts occur only for electromagnetic waves and not for mechanical sound waves, even though the passage states that beam angle was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure conflicts with the stated relationship, Δf is proportional to v cosθ when wave speed and transmitted frequency are fixed, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that measured frequency shift increased approximately linearly with flow speed and states that beam angle, fluid composition, probe frequency, and sound-speed calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.D: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls conflicts with the stated relationship, Δf is proportional to v cosθ when wave speed and transmitted frequency are fixed, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that measured frequency shift increased approximately linearly with flow speed and states that beam angle, fluid composition, probe frequency, and sound-speed calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

questions.stem: Which follow-up prediction is most consistent with the same C/P reasoning?

choices.C: The same numerical response should occur even if the manipulated variable is reversed
choices.D: The relationship would not apply because the system was modeled in a biological or clinical context

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.C: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that beam angle was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
choices.D: Doppler measurements relate observed frequency differences to relative motion along the wave path

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated an airway tube model driven by a speaker to identify resonant frequencies as the effective tube length was changed to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that resonant frequencies depend on wavelength, wave speed, boundary conditions, and tube length. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as v = fλ, and allowed wavelengths are set by the tube length and open/closed boundaries. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved increasing effective tube length while keeping air temperature and boundary condition constant. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: the first resonant frequency decreased as tube length increased. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that air temperature, speaker amplitude, tube diameter, and microphone calibration were held constant. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that longer tubes resonate at higher frequencies because the wave has more space to accelerate. Another was that resonance depends only on amplitude and not on wavelength or tube length. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to airway standing-wave model, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the airway standing-wave model experiment?

choices.A: Longer tubes resonate at higher frequencies because the wave has more space to accelerate
choices.B: Resonance depends only on amplitude and not on wavelength or tube length
choices.C: The lower resonance is best explained by longer allowed wavelengths in the longer tube
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Longer tubes resonate at higher frequencies because the wave has more space to accelerate conflicts with the stated relationship, v = fλ, and allowed wavelengths are set by the tube length and open/closed boundaries, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that the first resonant frequency decreased as tube length increased and states that air temperature, speaker amplitude, tube diameter, and microphone calibration were held constant. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.C: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as v = fλ, and allowed wavelengths are set by the tube length and open/closed boundaries, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, the first resonant frequency decreased as tube length increased, while air temperature, speaker amplitude, tube diameter, and microphone calibration were held constant. That pattern supports this choice because The lower resonance is best explained by longer allowed wavelengths in the longer tube. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Standing waves arise when boundary conditions permit stable interference patterns at particular wavelengths
choices.B: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that air temperature was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
